profile.name: Jesse Chen
profile.headline: Building technology that solves real problems for real people
profile.description: Computer science student at Carnegie Mellon building secure mobile, AI, and full-stack systems that addresses meaningful challenges.

experiences.summary: Developing Android/Kotlin mobile features in an Agile healthcare engineering workflow, translating Jira tickets into sprint-scoped implementation tasks and production-ready deliverables.

Launched a LangGraph/FastAPI campus AI platform at 1.4s p95 first-token latency as the top code contributor, routing OpenRouter LLMs through grounded MCP tools, SSE streaming, and sandboxed outputs. Built the React client, surfacing content 81% sooner and cutting UI updates 90% via frame batching. Engineered persistent agent memory recalling user context across chats via pgvector and injection-resistant controls.

Shipped core features across 36 of 41 screens in a 10K+ user Flutter app. Engineered a perceptual color engine to recommend the best shade for each user's skin tone, sampling swatch imagery, converting sRGB to CIELAB, and ranking each product's shade lineup by Delta-E in under 0.005ms. Cut homepage load time ~75% via parallel API fan-out, feed prefetching, token pagination, and TTL-cached revisits.

Built the ~350ms geospatial ETL pipeline powering a CMU campus navigation site for 700+ monthly users, fusing 15K+ OpenStreetMap nodes with ArcGIS metadata into footprints, floors, facility IDs, and entrances.

items.label: cmumaps · map
items.alt: CMUMaps campus map with building pins

experiences.summary: Built Unreal Engine 5 Blueprint gameplay systems for grappling, tethering, and local multiplayer combat, synchronizing physics-driven interactions, collision feedback, and elimination state in real time.
Designed and taught project-based Python, C++, and JavaScript curriculum centered on algorithms and competitive problem solving through structured implementation labs.

Developed and evaluated a computer-vision model for Parkinson's exercise-form assessment, achieving 95.4% mean accuracy on controlled exercise videos and co-authoring a peer-reviewed paper presented at CS & IT-CSCP 2024.

projects.name: Agoriai
projects.lead: An anonymous student career network built in one TartanHacks weekend with a typed React client, Bun/Elysia API, Drizzle, PostgreSQL, and privacy controls enforced at the server boundary.
bullets: Converted roughly 10K relationship signals into about 2K weighted graph edges at 16ms p95 API latency while preserving server-side anonymity.
Added school-email authentication, transactional voting, recursive discussions, and evasion-resistant moderation.

caseStudy.challenge: Career networks are useful only when students can discover relevant peers without exposing private identity or academic context. The system needed to preserve anonymity while still supporting search, discussion, and relationship exploration.
approach: Enforced four identity-visibility tiers on the server rather than trusting the client.
Modeled student-company relationships as weighted edges and rendered them through a D3 force-directed graph.
Normalized leetspeak and applied fuzzy matching before moderation decisions.

projects.name: ParkiWell
projects.lead: An offline-first Flutter care platform for Parkinson’s patients with journaled writes, idempotent deduplication, parallel reads, and delta sync.
bullets: Validated zero data loss across 100K fault-injected writes while cutting sync time ~80%.
Built on-device analytics linking 10K medication and therapy events to longitudinal symptom trends.

caseStudy.challenge: Care tracking cannot become unavailable when connectivity drops, and reconnecting must never duplicate or erase health records.
approach: Recorded offline mutations in a journal and replayed them with idempotent deduplication.
Used versioned snapshots and delta sync to minimize transfer and recovery time.
Protected every hosted table with row-level security and shipped signed builds through CI/CD.

projects.name: Tarocchi
projects.lead: An interactive narrative web experience with 24 branching story paths, replayable state-driven progression, parallax, and synchronized audio.
bullets: Modeled route and scene state explicitly so branching flows remain coherent as narrative complexity grows.
Built a reusable presentation pipeline for animated transitions, layered parallax, and synchronized sound.

caseStudy.challenge: Twenty-four story branches had to feel cinematic without allowing animation, audio, and route state to drift apart.
approach: Represented narrative progress as explicit state instead of coupling it to visual components.
Synchronized scene transitions and audio cues from the same route events.
Used reusable parallax and motion primitives to keep visual behavior consistent.

projects.name: MyCommunity
projects.lead: A native Java Android platform for Boy Scouts troop discovery with live Firebase data, Google Maps, runtime location permissions, and Google Sign-In.
bullets: Bound live troop updates to map markers and validated authenticated profile CRUD.
Streamed and cached a community news feed with OkHttp, Gson, and Glide.
terminal: nyt api ▸ feed cached via glide

caseStudy.challenge: Troop discovery data had to stay current on a location-aware map while respecting Android’s authentication and runtime permission model.
approach: Bound Firebase listeners to map marker lifecycle and profile state.
Handled location permission and Google Sign-In as explicit application states.
Fetched community news asynchronously and cached images for responsive browsing.
